title: Twelve local hubs, one year, and what really happened.
dek: In late 2025 the Localism Fund backed twelve community hubs across six continents to run their own local grant programs — using Ethereum as the rails. This is the honest retrospective: what each one set out to do, what they actually did, and what the money really touched.
intro: The Localism Fund exists on a simple bet: that the people closest to a place are the ones best placed to regenerate it — and that open, on-chain money can move trust and resources to them without a middle layer skimming the meaning out.

Round 01 put that bet to work. Rather than fund projects directly, the Fund funded twelve local hubs to each run their own grant program — choosing local projects, moving money, and reporting back. Each received roughly $8,000–$18,000 and four months. The brief: distribute it locally, on Ethereum, and tell us honestly what happened.

Four months on, every hub submitted a report. We read all twelve against their original applications, traced the money on-chain where we could, and wrote an evaluation for each. What follows is a project-by-project account — grockable at a glance, with the full evidence a click away.

findings.lede: The headline isn't the spending. It's the gap between designing a local grant round and actually running one.
paragraphs: Most of these hubs are real, active, locally-rooted projects doing genuine work — solar installs in wartime Ukraine, gardeners paid in Rome, savings circles in Dar es Salaam, ten landscapes telling their own story in Cascadia. The energy is real.

But the specific thing Round 01 funded — an open local grant round that moves money to independent local projects on-chain — often didn't execute as designed. In several cases the grant still sits intact in a treasury months later. In others it paid for the hub's own build or operations rather than a round of local grants. In a few, the reported numbers simply didn't reconcile against the chain.

A handful delivered the real thing cleanly: GEN Ukraine funded eleven ecovillage projects, fully traced on-chain; the ReFi Design Sprint paid a verified grant to a community gardening cooperative. The rest sit somewhere between intention and delivery — which is exactly what a first round is for surfacing.

We've kept the account fair and specific. A thin report from a strong project, and a polished report from a stalled one, are both possible — so each story separates what the program did from how well it was reported, and says plainly where the evidence runs out.

byNumbers.label: ran a clean local round, fully on-chain
grants still largely held in treasury

insights.lead: Running a local round is harder than funding one.
insights.body: Most hubs did real local work; far fewer completed an open round that actually reached independent local projects on-chain.

insights.lead: Treasuries filled, but didn't always empty.
insights.body: In several cases the grant still sits intact in a Safe months after payout — the money arrived, the local round didn't.
More than one hub counted its own prior grant or a standing treasury as new co-funding raised for the round.

insights.lead: On-chain verification only works when there's something on-chain.
insights.body: Where disbursements were fiat, undeployed, or routed to the hub's own wallets, the claims couldn't be independently checked.

insights.lead: The cleanest delivery came from existing trust.
insights.body: GEN Ukraine's ecovillage network and a Rome gardening co-op moved fastest — relationships, not tooling, carried the round.

tierSummary.note: real work; meaningful gaps to resolve

method: Each project was evaluated twice. An AI evaluation worked through four phases — structural completeness, evidence and on-chain financial verification, substantive assessment against the application, and a synthesis with a composite score out of 35. Every financial finding was then independently re-verified by a second, adversarial pass before being committed.

A steward companion sits alongside each one, carrying the human judgement and the follow-up questions the Fund will actually send. Both are still drafts: this site is a working retrospective, not a final verdict.
Where a block explorer wouldn't resolve, we say so rather than guess. "We couldn't verify this" and "the evidence contradicts this" are kept strictly apart throughout.
